Summary: This study develops and validates a radiomics-based machine learning model to predict the EGFR T790M mutation in NSCLC patients at diagnosis using CT images. The model integrates radiomic features and clinical factors, achieving an AUC of 0.86 in predicting the T790M mutation, which can aid in targeted treatment planning.

Condition1.reason: The study includes manual segmentation of tumor regions of interest (ROIs) on CT images.

Condition2.reason: The study mentions manual segmentation of the tumor ROIs, indicating that the segmentation is not fully automated.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts 1316 radiomics features from CT images, which are hand-crafted features.

Condition4.reason: The study uses tabular data, including radiomic features and clinical features, in a structured format for analysis.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning approach. Instead, it relies on hand-crafted radiomic features and machine learning models.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines.

Item2.reason: The study defines clear inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection, ensuring a representative study population.

Item3.reason: The study uses re-biopsy and ARMS-PCR or NGS to detect the presence of T790M mutation, which is a robust reference standard.

Item4.reason: The study involves data from a single institution, the First Affiliated Hospital of Shandong First Medical University.

Item5.reason: The study uses CT imaging, which is a standard imaging technique for NSCLC, and reports the use of mediastinal and lung window settings.

Item6.reason: The study reports the CT acquisition parameters, including tube voltage, tube current, detector collimation, field of view, pitch, and matrix size.

Item7.reason: The study uses CT scans performed prior to any treatment for lung cancer, ensuring the imaging data reflects the baseline condition before TKI therapy.

Item8.reason: The study describes the manual segmentation process performed by experienced radiologists using ITK-SNAP, with verification by another radiologist.

Item9.reason: The study uses manual segmentation, not fully automated segmentation, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The study mentions that the tumor regions were manually segmented by a senior radiologist, reflecting clinical practice.

Item11.reason: The study describes the image preprocessing techniques, including resampling to standardize image representations and converting pixel values to Hounsfield Units (HU).

Item12.reason: The study uses the Pyradiomics library for feature extraction, which is compliant with the Image Biomarker Standardization Initiative (IBSI).

Item13.reason: The study describes the radiomic features extracted, including first-order statistics, shape-based features, and textural features (GLCM, GLRLM, GLSZM, GLDM, NGTDM). It also mentions the use of wavelet and Laplacian of Gaussian filters.

Item14.reason: The study calculates the intra-group correlation coefficient (ICC) to assess the consistency of segmentation between two radiologists and retains only features with an ICC greater than 0.85.

Item15.reason: The study uses the Wilcoxon rank sum test and the LASSO algorithm to select potential features related to T790M mutational status.

Item16.reason: The study does not provide a clear justification for the appropriateness of the dimensionality compared to the data size using statistical means or empirical evidence.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study randomly assigns patients to a training cohort and a validation cohort.

Item19.reason: The study analyzes potential confounding factors such as age, gender, smoking status, stage of disease, and serum level of tumor markers.

Item20.reason: The study uses appropriate accuracy metrics such as AUC, sensitivity, and specificity to evaluate the performance of the models.

Item21.reason: The study reports 95% confidence intervals (CI) for the AUC values.

Item22.reason: The study includes a calibration curve to assess the predictive ability of the model.

Item23.reason: The study does not explicitly compare the performance of uni-parametric imaging with multi-parametric imaging to justify the need for a multi-parametric approach.

Item24.reason: The study does not include a non-radiomic method for comparison purposes. It does not compare the radiomics model with a clinical standard or other established method.

Item25.reason: The study does not compare the machine learning models with a simple baseline reference model.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an independent validation cohort sampled from the same source as the training set.

Item27.reason: The study does not include external testing with independent data from other institutions.

Item28.reason: The authors state that the original data supporting the conclusions of this paper will be provided unreservedly by the authors to any qualified researcher and that the datasets used and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author.

Item29.reason: The study does not mention the availability of the code used for radiomics analysis.

Item30.reason: The study does not mention the availability of the final model.
